perturbation.original_text: 18.1 The terms of this Agreement shall be one year from the effective date and will be automatically renewed on each anniversary of the effective date, for a renewal term of one year unless either party provides written notification of its intention not to renew this Agreement at least one month prior to the expiration of the initial or any renewal terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 18.1 The terms of this Agreement shall be one year from the effective date and will be automatically renewed on each anniversary of the effective date, for a renewal term of one year unless either party provides written notification of its intention not to renew this Agreement at least two weeks prior to the expiration of the initial or any renewal terms.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the non-renewal notice period to two weeks may be insufficient under California law, which often requires longer notice periods for contract terminations, especially those involving significant business relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While a simple non-renewal notice isn't a direct restraint, reducing the notice period to two weeks, especially in a distributorship agreement, can be viewed as an unreasonable restraint if it doesn't allow the distributor adequate time to find alternative arrangements, potentially violating the spirit of this section if it effectively forces them out of business. Common law contract principles dictate reasonable notice, and 2 weeks may be seen as not enough notice in certain situations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-renewal notice period from one month to two weeks, which the Law Explanation suggests may be insufficient, potentially violating the spirit of California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, particularly if it doesn't allow the distributor adequate time to find alternative arrangements. The law snippets (BPC 16600) state that contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business are void. While not a direct restraint, the insufficient notice period could indirectly restrain a distributor's business if they are not given enough time to prepare, creating a legal contradiction under Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.7 If Tripath notifies Distributor of any discrepancy in a credit request, Distributor has one month to resolve the discrepancy and pay the amount of the discrepancy.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.7 If Tripath notifies Distributor of any discrepancy in a credit request, Distributor has one week to resolve the discrepancy and pay the amount of the discrepancy.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the discrepancy resolution period to one week could be problematic if the distributor needs more time to investigate complex billing issues or gather supporting documentation. This is particularly so considering that California law requires fairness in business practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2309 states that where a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time. What is reasonable is fact specific and it does not give explicit numbers. Shortening the time frame to one week to resolve any discrepancy is not reasonable in the commercial context.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the discrepancy resolution period from one month to one week. The Law Explanation states that shortening the time frame to one week is not reasonable in the commercial context. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2309 (1) states that if the time for any action under a contract is not provided, it should be a reasonable time. Therefore, changing the time from one month to one week for discrepancy resolution could be considered unreasonable and contradict the requirement for a reasonable time frame, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 Distributor's appointment is for a term of one year from the Effective Date, renewable automatically unless terminated under Section 18
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 Distributor's appointment is for a term of one year from the Effective Date, renewable automatically, with no right to terminate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to terminate creates an issue regarding contract enforceability and potential violation of California's laws on contracts of adhesion.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 deals with unconscionable contracts or clauses. Removing the right to terminate makes the agreement potentially unconscionable, especially if the distributor makes significant investments based on the agreement. A court could refuse to enforce the contract or limit the application of the unconscionable clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the distributor's right to terminate, which introduces a potential issue of unconscionability under California Civil Code Section 1670.5, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes California Civil Code Section 1670.5, stating that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. By eliminating the termination clause, the contract could be seen as excessively one-sided, especially if the distributor invests heavily in the agreement, thus creating a strong contradiction with the principle of fair contracts.
